title: BES Cyber System Information Access via Share and Web Downloads

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CIP-011-3 R1 Part 1.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NERC CIP CIP-011-3 R1 Part 1.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.13.57: BES Cyber System Information Access via Share and Web Downloads.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.13.57' running on index=wineventlog EventCode=5145 or proxy logs, bcsi_path_tags.csv, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=5145 earliest=-24h
| lookup bcsi_path_tags.csv ShareName, RelativeTargetName OUTPUT classification
| where classification="BCSI"
| stats sum(TransferredBytes) as bytes by Account_Name, ShareName, ComputerName
| where bytes > 104857600
| sort - bytes
description: We monitor access to file shares and web paths classified as BES Cyber System Information for inappropriate bulk retrieval.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NERC CIP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes wineventlog and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Tag BCSI paths in lookup from information protection register. (2) Enable detailed file share auditing. (3) Alert on high-byte transfers. (4) Integrate with insider threat program. Indexes required: `index=wineventlog`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security`.
visualization: Table (large transfers), Bar chart (by user), Single value (events). Key fields: `Account_Name`, `ComputerName`, `ShareName`, `bytes`.
knownFalsePositives: Planned maintenance, registered change tickets, and approved work can look like noncompliance in raw telemetry; confirm against the current change record before you escalate.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=wineventlog` `EventCode=5145` or proxy logs, `bcsi_path_tags.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Tag BCSI paths in lookup from information protection register. (2) Enable detailed file share auditing. (3) Alert on high-byte transfers. (4) Integrate with insider threat program.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" EventCode=5145 earliest=-24h
| lookup bcsi_path_tags.csv ShareName, RelativeTargetName OUTPUT classification
| where classification="BCSI"
| stats sum(TransferredBytes) as bytes by Account_Name, ShareName, ComputerName
| where bytes > 104857600
| sort - bytes
```

Understanding this SPL

**BES Cyber System Information Access via Share and Web Downloads (CIP-011-3 R1 Part 1.1)** — We monitor access to file shares and web paths classified as BES Cyber System Information for inappropriate bulk retrieval.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=wineventlog` `EventCode=5145` or proxy logs, `bcsi_path_tags.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: wineventlog; **sourcetype**: WinEventLog:Security. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=wineventlog, sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where classification="BCSI"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by Account_Name, ShareName, ComputerName** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where bytes > 104857600` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the official BES or cyber-asset list, the ESP edge firewall, and the same Windows or network data this search is built to mirror for that time window. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (large transfers), Bar chart (by user), Single value (events)
grandmaExplanation: We monitor access to file shares and web paths classified as BES Cyber System Information for inappropriate bulk retrieval. That is important for the big power grid, where proof needs to line up with what the rules expect.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Authentication data model (Authentication.Authentication).
